summary.intent: Add local-vs-fork session detection to the Gossamer VS Code extension, distinguishing whether a Claude session's working directory exists on the current machine. Simultaneously, fix server lifecycle management so multiple VS Code windows can share one Gossamer server instance.
summary.outcome: A `/health` endpoint with `SERVER_API_VERSION` was added to `src/server.ts` and `bundled-server/serve.js`, and `GossamerPanel.ts` was updated to reuse a compatible server or kill-and-respawn an outdated one. However, the cold-start case (no server running) was still broken when the session ended at the rate limit.

repo: The extension ships two server scripts: `packages/vscode/bundled-server/serve.js` (pre-bundled, used from VSIX) takes priority over `dist/serve.js` (compiled from `src/server.ts`, used in dev mode). Both must be kept in sync manually when adding server-side features.

The extension is loaded and tested from the `gossamer-testing` git worktree, which has its own build output. Changes to the main repo are not reflected there until that worktree is separately rebuilt.

`spawnServer()` was fire-and-forget in the constructor — running concurrently with `waitForServer()` — which caused a race: `waitForServer` could resolve on a stale server that `spawnServer` was about to kill.
`probeServer` originally checked `/api/sessions` to detect a running server; the new `probeServerVersion` checks `/health` for a version-tagged response `{v: 2}`.
`SERVER_API_VERSION` must be manually duplicated in `bundled-server/serve.js` since that file is a pre-built artifact, not compiled from source at extension build time.
esbuild minifies all function names in `packages/vscode/dist/extension.js`; verifying bundle contents requires searching for string literals (e.g., `"/health"`) rather than function names.

code.finding: spawnServer() was fire-and-forget; waitForServer() racing it caused the panel to connect to a stale server before it was killed. Fix: await spawnServer() before starting waitForServer().
Added GET /health endpoint returning { v: SERVER_API_VERSION } so the extension can distinguish compatible from incompatible running servers.
Manually patched to add the same /health endpoint as src/server.ts; this file is a pre-built artifact and does not get regenerated by npm run build.

workflow: When `npm run build -w packages/vscode` is run, only the extension bundle is rebuilt — `dist/serve.js` and `dist/server.js` (compiled from `src/server.ts`) are NOT updated. A full `npm run build` or explicit `tsc` is needed for the server.

Popping a git stash that also touched `bundled-server/serve.js` silently makes that pre-built artifact diverge from `src/server.ts`; always check stash contents with `git stash show -p` before popping.
SIGTERM with a fixed 500ms wait is unreliable for releasing a TCP port; polling until the port is free (or using SIGKILL) is more robust.

friction: The stash contained changes to `bundled-server/serve.js` that weren't immediately obvious, causing multiple red-herring debugging rounds before the artifact divergence was spotted.
The `gossamer-testing` worktree had a stale build throughout much of the session, so the 'broken plugin' being tested didn't reflect the actual code changes — this was only caught late.
`npm run build` does not rebuild `dist/serve.js` from `src/server.ts` in a predictable way; it took several commands to confirm which output file had the new `/health` route.
A lingering compatible server on port 3456 (from a Claude Code session) masked whether the extension's spawn-or-reuse logic was working correctly.
The session hit a rate limit before the cold-start regression was resolved.
open_items: Cold-start bug unresolved: when no server is running on the port, the plugin fails to connect after the spawnServer→waitForServer chain changes. Root cause not identified before session ended.
SERVER_API_VERSION must be manually kept in sync between src/server.ts and packages/vscode/bundled-server/serve.js — no automated check or build step enforces this.
The gossamer-testing worktree rebuild process is manual and error-prone; consider a watch/sync script or a documented rebuild step in CLAUDE.md.
SIGKILL vs SIGTERM: switched to SIGKILL for the kill-before-respawn path, but the interaction with a server that has open DB connections or file watchers was not evaluated.
